does: This paper presents EvalStop, a scheduler-level early stopping mechanism that detects and halts reward model optimization when downstream evaluation metrics plateau or decline, indicating overoptimization. The core contribution is a composable primitive that monitors world feedback signals during multi-tenant RLHF fine-tuning, terminates jobs after k consecutive eval-score drops, preserves the best checkpoint, and releases GPU resources back to the scheduler. The authors frame this as a detection problem and evaluate EvalStop using a discrete-event simulator on synthetic RLHF workloads containing both reward-hacking and healthy runs, demonstrating that the approach can identify divergence between proxy reward models and actual quality signals without requiring human intervention.
connects: This work directly addresses the reward overoptimization problem identified by Gao et al., building on concerns about proxy reward model divergence under sustained optimization pressure in RLHF systems. It extends prior research on platform-level scheduling for LLM fine-tuning, specifically critiquing existing approaches: non-clairvoyant schedulers that optimize only job completion time, quality-aware variants like SLAQ that rely on training loss as a weaker proxy, and classical per-job early stopping that requires manual oversight. The paper integrates insights from reinforcement learning from human feedback literature with practical cloud computing constraints, positioning scheduler-level early stopping as a missing mechanism between individual job management and platform-wide resource allocation.
enables: EvalStop sets up future work on automated quality-aware scheduling for LLM fine-tuning platforms by establishing a framework for detecting optimization failures without human oversight. It enables more robust multi-tenant resource allocation where GPUs can be dynamically freed from diverging jobs, improving platform efficiency and preventing wasted computation. The composability of the primitive suggests extensions to other proxy optimization problems beyond RLHF and opportunities for more sophisticated detection strategies that combine multiple eval signals. This work also points toward developing better ground-truth feedback mechanisms for cloud platforms and opens questions about optimal threshold selection for eval-score decline detection across diverse workload distributions.
